chapters.title: “Negative” doesn’t always mean “bad”
chapters.text:
We are the sum of people around us and everything good and bad they have to offer. Unfortunately, the pain of being hurt is usually stronger than all the happy butterflies in our stomachs; wounds also leave scars for us never to forget. But while remembering can avert us from repeating a hurtful mistake, forgiving seems like thanking for that punch. Who in their sound mind would've done that, right?!

Whether we tell ourselves we have moved on, the weight of unresolved trauma continues to slam on our anchors. This kind of self-conceit usually leads nowhere, so we need to learn to process it for our own healing.

Facing your fears can help you escape the loop of being repeatedly hurt.

Forgiving and compassion toward others start with self-understanding, which you can develop through RAIN — Recognizing, Allowing, Investigating, and Nurturing. You'll learn to take a serious, thoughtful look at your feelings, accept them, and take unconditional care of yourself. The moment it becomes your regular practice, the silent storm of RAIN will catch those around you. So, relax and let it rain!

chapters.title: It can’t rain all the time
chapters.text:
The life rhythm has never been as dynamic as today — so much to do in so little time! You are so preoccupied with your work or relationships that you rarely have time to stop and think about what you're doing and how it makes you feel.

You share the same brain with your ancestors, who had significantly less business to stress about…besides being eaten. Jokes aside, it is no wonder human consciousness shifts to autopilot just to get people through the day. Ultimately, you're left with hanging anxieties and emotionally charged stories that might be untrue.

Trance is the opposite of being aware of your feelings and how they make you act and is often accompanied by body stiffness, absent-mindedness, or disassociation. However, the most challenging feature is reactiveness, when you use your emotions to deal with negative experiences instead of your present consciousness. As expected, your feelings cannot solve the problem — they only bottle it up for "better times," hence the trance or lack of awareness.

Leaving disorder can be terrifying when it's the only order you know.

This "bottling" problem has long been under philosophers’ radar. Take Buddhist teacher Michele McDonald, who, in the 1980s, developed a therapeutic approach to mental uncorking called RAIN. RAIN stands for four steps toward inner healing: Recognize, Allow, Investigate, and Nurture. However, the immediate goal of this approach is to exit the trance and return to the present.

Recognizing a negative thought — be it anxiety or fear — is the first step. Pause, sit comfortably, and close your eyes, followed by a few deep breaths. With each exhale, release the tension occupying your body. Be mindful of your sensations and emotions and allow them to be, even when they're uncomfortable. This skill will help you wake up from trance and automatic reactions. Then, as you invite your hurtful feelings in, investigate what caused them by asking yourself:
1. Have I felt like this before?
2. What part of me hurts most?
3. What does this part need to unwind?

Finally, when the need is clear, spend some time being compassionate with yourself and nurturing the part of you that's hurting. Start by imagining your younger self and then ensure that child that you're here for them.

chapters.title: “Negative” doesn’t always mean “bad”
chapters.text:
One of the "side effects" of being part of society is the negative self-beliefs that develop as a survival response. Your biological makeup made your ancestors prioritize negative experiences over positive ones for survival, but this feature is outdated and occasionally harmful today.

Your beliefs live in your thoughts and feelings; you can assume them to be valid because they are more emotionally charged than rational talk. But being real and being true are two different things. Imagine you're juggling a job, a child, and an elderly parent needing constant care. It is only a matter of time before you get overwhelmed and annoyed with all three, thinking, "I wish it all didn't exist" or "I'm all but worth their love."

Compassion is the ultimate medicine for negative self-talk; it reminds you of your humanity.

The truth is that your beliefs depend on your emotions, which are known for being fleeting and responsive — anything but true. Negative beliefs are real because they mirror your mental well-being in real time, but they don't reflect your personality. This entity doesn't change from an occasional burst of anger.

How often do you feel anxious about acting "unlike you" before others, risking criticism? Unpleasant pictures of people-pleasing may start popping in your head right now.

According to Buddhist psychology, a part of freedom is the state of "no self," which is being unlimited in paths you want to try and free of outside judgment. It is also your final stop of the RAIN journey. Having said that, fighting a sense of unworthiness goes like this:
1. Position yourself comfortably and collect your focus with a few deep breaths.
2. Recall a belief that made you suffer or ask yourself, "What am I believing?" if you're hurting right now.
3. Visualize the situation in detail: What and who do you see? What were your thoughts and feelings at the moment?
4. Allow the belief to just be there.
5. Ask yourself: "Is this true?" and "Am I sure it's true?"
6. Note how having this belief has affected your life and creativity.
7. Invite your future self to nurture you. You're still here for yourself.

If the idea of your future self doesn't help, try turning to your partner or family, praying, or retreating to nature. See what works best for you.

chapters.title: The happiness in the corners of your mind
chapters.text:
Practicing RAIN takes time and effort. Perfect conditions seldom exist to help you relax and return to your senses. Most of the time, anxiety and fear stand between you and RAIN's peacefulness like a giant menacing guard.

Practicing calm or encouraging self-talk seems untimely when your erratic boss is waiting for your comment or your family member is going through personal problems. It may look flighty to someone, but in reality, these moments are perfect for exercising RAIN, though it may differ slightly from the original.

It's hard to deny that the modern lifestyle is like spluttering oil when the brain is a hot pan. Experiencing too much pressure causes your brain's prefrontal cortex to fail to down-regulate itself, resulting in bursts of anger. Regular stress makes your "window of tolerance," a concept created by the psychiatrist Daniel Siegel, too narrow, so you become more prone to mental trauma.

Sensitivity teaches you to appreciate happiness by putting you against life's sharp corners.

Fear is natural for your survival as a human being but can also restrain you from self-development. However, resisting negative emotions also means avoiding joyous experiences, so try to remind yourself of this the next time you refuse to face your fear — it can make fighting your anxiety more manageable.

Standing up to whatever paralyzes you takes intention. Furthermore, it's usually a lonely journey because no one can feel and understand you better than you can. That's why nurture is the most significant part of fighting anxiety. Although nurture is usually RAIN's final step, sometimes you need some self-soothing first to create a safe mental space for effective RAIN.

Start with recollecting moments when you felt safe and happy, like pleasant tiredness after a busy day, appreciating the result of your work, or enjoying your friends’ company. If you struggle to do so, mentally record such moments when they happen and repeat the procedure; later, you can refer to them to soothe yourself before practicing RAIN, that is, recognizing and accepting your painful emotions, investigating their interior origins, and nurturing yourself to act despite them.

We are not the survival of the fittest, we are the survival of the nurtured. ~ Louis Cozolino

Did you know? According to a 2020 study, mindfulness positively impacts the brain’s pathways that process information from your five senses.

chapters.title: Taking the plunge into forgiveness
chapters.text:
Imagine it's your last day on Earth, and you're looking back at your life. What would you want to see — loving and being loved or marinating in grudges and wounds?

Life allows occasional bruises, but it is up to you to let them heal or soak more deeply into your heart.

Post-conflict anger can create a fog between you and the other person, making you act emotionally from a place of pain.

Let's look at the example of a son (Mike) whose father (John) constantly picked on him as a child. Mike has to take care of John after moving him to an assisted living center. Angry with his father, Mike is not ready to forgive; all he remembers are John’s insults and the subsequent pain that keeps them apart. In a situation like this, slipping into a victim mindset is very simple, but it keeps the person from releasing pain. Eventually, John risks never being honest about his feelings, while Mike may develop a distorted vision of parenthood. In other words, they become prisoners of their unresolved pain.

If you are clinging to old traumas like Mike and John, ask yourself: "What weakness am I afraid to expose if I try to forgive?" Many people will list reasons like:
• If that person is not wrong, then I must be wrong.
• I'd have to take responsibility for my words and actions.
• Forgiving means accepting the insult.

However, forgiving is anything but suppressing your anger or heartache or becoming "inferior" to the other person. It is an intense process consisting of three steps:
1. Being intentional: Forgiveness is a tough choice, but it frees your heartspace for better people. Try thinking about it as cleaning your search history for better functionality.
2. Include RAIN: Anger with someone can indicate a mental fog concealing your vulnerability. You must recognize fears like the ones mentioned earlier, allow them to be, study their origins, and nurture yourself like the person who hurt you couldn't.
3. Forgive: Aggression often comes from fear of being hurt again. But being aware of it and nurturing yourself into a safe place helps release the mental clench you had on the other person.

chapters.title: This bird has my wings
chapters.text:
One thing that is more challenging than forgiveness is compassion. Whether you insulted or were offended intentionally, it takes courage to see the situation from an outside perspective and be compassionate to another person.

You just witnessed an example of Recognition, the first step of RAIN; you can practice this by actively observing other people's moods, energy, and behavior — both good and bad. Especially the latter, as negative reactions often unveil people's vulnerabilities, just as they hold the key to compassion. Denying them won't be suitable for any party — you can only allow them to be here for the time being.

Compassion is a firm muscle, while empathy is not; both can carry heavy luggage but they exhaust differently.

You can also practice Recognition and Allowing with your loved ones. Sit face-to-face with your knees inches away from each other and close your eyes. Breathe deeply and relax for some time; then open your eyes and exchange each other's silent observations for five minutes. Just be there and grant your pure attention. In the end, share what you felt and experienced.

The reason to sit with the other person's feelings, even unsaid ones, is to:
• Learn to be more understanding of your feelings and address them without judgment.
• Investigate their nature afterward.
• Take accountability where it's due.

Overall, facing your or others' bitterness can be mentally draining, which is the dark side of empathy — the skill everyone is encouraged to practice today. However, compassion is not as demanding; it enables observation. You're likely sympathetic if someone hurts your feelings, but you can see the old wound behind their aggression. Compassion doesn't require your help cutting the emotional knot or leveling with those who hurt you.

What it does require is knowing yourself enough to identify others' negative feelings when they're triggered. Think of the last time you were angry with someone, and ask yourself these questions:
• What mood was I in?
• How did I feel physically?
• Was I frustrated about something else then?
• Did I feel tense or relaxed when talking?
• Was the interaction difficult?

These questions can help you see a vulnerable human being behind the facade.

Wisdom tells me I am nothing. Love tells me I am everything. And between the two, my life flows. ~ Sri Nisargadatta

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Maturing involves growing up physically while also becoming mentally stronger. Learning to forgive and let go is challenging and sometimes tedious, but also exhilarating.

Even though working out can make your muscles feel weak, we all know that it's actually making them stronger. The same can be said about compassion — you may feel like you're negotiating with someone who hurt you, thus being the weaker one, when in reality, you're learning to be resilient. So, remember this mantra whenever life throws you lemons — vitamin C is good for your health!

Try this

• Forgiving yourself is the hardest, so use RAIN to start your compassion journey.
• Avoiding intense emotions can result in inward and outward anger, so give them space to be by writing them down or recording a voice memo; later, when you're better, you can investigate them.
• Develop your awareness of personal compassion: Pay attention when sympathy is easy or difficult for you, like watching the news or seeing a homeless person.
• Set an intention for the next week. For example, help an elderly neighbor, visit a pet shelter, or try another way to practice compassion.
